possible_causes: Overbaking - the cake was left in the oven too long
Too much flour - measuring flour by scooping instead of weighing
Not enough liquid - insufficient milk, oil, or eggs
High altitude baking - requires recipe adjustments
Inadequate cooling - storing while still warm causes moisture loss

fixes.details: Use a toothpick inserted in the center - it should have a few moist crumbs, not be completely dry. Reduce baking time by 5-10 minutes next time.
Use a kitchen scale (best) or spoon and level method. Don't scoop directly into the bag. Fluffy flour = air = lighter cake.
Brush the cake with simple syrup (1:1 sugar and water), milk, or apple juice. This restores moisture while still warm.
Let cake cool completely on a wire rack before wrapping. Cover with plastic wrap or place in an airtight container to retain moisture.
Use a rich frosting or filling (buttercream, cream cheese frosting) to add moisture and flavor.

next_time_tips: Add one extra egg yolk for more moisture
Replace half the milk with sour cream or yogurt
Add pudding mix to the batter for extra moisture
Use oil instead of butter (oil distributes moisture better)
Add applesauce or mashed banana for natural moisture

possible_causes: Expired or incorrect leavening agents - baking soda/powder loses potency
Insufficient mixing - dry ingredients not properly combined with wet
Oven too cold - temperature wasn't preheated or maintained
Too much liquid - batter too thin to hold air bubbles
Opened oven door too early - released trapped heat and moisture

fixes.details: Baking powder has a shelf life of 6 months to 2 years. Baking soda lasts 5-10 years but check if it smells fresh. Test by adding to vinegar - it should fizz vigorously.
Turn on oven 15-20 minutes before baking. Use an oven thermometer to verify it reaches the correct temperature.
Cream butter and sugar until light and fluffy (2-3 minutes). Gently fold in dry ingredients to maintain air. Don't overmix after adding flour.
Don't open oven door until at least 2/3 through baking time. Sudden temperature changes collapse the cake structure.
If you must peek, open gently and quickly. Opening releases heat and moisture that the cake needs to rise.

next_time_tips: Replace 1/4 cup flour with cornstarch for a lighter cake
Add cream of tartar to baking soda (1:2 ratio)
Separate eggs - beat whites until stiff peaks, fold in last
Use fresh baking powder, not baking soda alone
Don't skip preheating the oven

possible_causes: Oven temperature too high - running hotter than the set temperature
Baking time too long - left in oven past the intended time
Wrong rack position - too close to heating element
Dark pans - absorb more heat than light pans
Crowded oven - blocks air circulation and causes uneven heating

fixes.details: If only the edges are burnt, carefully trim them off with a serrated knife. If the whole cake is dark but not rock-hard, serve with frosting to mask the flavor.
Use an oven thermometer to check actual temperature. Many ovens run 25-50°F hotter or colder than displayed. Adjust recipes accordingly.
Use light-colored metal pans (aluminum or steel). Avoid dark or glass pans, which absorb more heat. Line bottom with parchment paper for protection.
Place cakes on the middle rack. This ensures even heat distribution above and below. Avoid the top and bottom racks which cook faster.
Start checking for doneness 5 minutes before the recipe time. Toothpick test: inserted in center should come out with a few moist crumbs, not wet.

next_time_tips: Reduce oven temperature by 25°F and increase baking time slightly
Use parchment paper on pan bottoms to prevent browning
Tent the cake with aluminum foil if browning too quickly (halfway through)
Bake at lower temperature for longer time rather than high temperature
Ensure oven racks have adequate space for air circulation

possible_causes: Insufficient baking time - removed from oven too early
Oven temperature too low - temperature not reached or lost
Incorrect toothpick test - didn't insert to center or tested wrong spot
Very wet batter - recipe requires more baking time than usual
Large cake size - needs more time for heat to reach center

fixes.action: Return to oven if still warm
fixes.details: If cake is just barely undercooked, return to oven for 5-10 more minutes. Check with toothpick in center - should come out clean or with moist crumbs only.
Use an oven thermometer. Many ovens run cooler than displayed. If yours does, increase temperature and bake longer.
Insert toothpick in the center (deepest part). For brownies, a few crumbs are OK. For cakes, should be mostly clean. If unsure, bake 2-3 more minutes.
Check internal temperature: cake is done at 200-210°F (93-99°C) for most cakes, 215-220°F (102-104°C) for denser cakes.
Don't rush! Each additional minute matters. It's better to slightly overbake than underbake. Slightly dry > wet and gooey.

next_time_tips: Use a cake thermometer for guaranteed results
Increase baking time by 10-15 minutes for every 2 inches of pan depth
Bake at lower temperature for longer time (prevents burning while ensuring even cooking)
Fill pans only 1/2 to 2/3 full for even baking
Check multiple spots - center, corners, and between edges

possible_causes: Oven temperature too high - causes rapid rise and cracking
Too much leavening - too much baking soda/powder causes over-rising
Batter too stiff - doesn't rise evenly, creates stress cracks
Rapid temperature change - opening oven door suddenly
Pan too small - batter rises too much and cracks

fixes.action: The cake is still fine to eat!
fixes.details: Cracks don't affect flavor or texture. Use frosting to hide cracks. A layer cake can be frosted between layers to conceal cracks completely.
Reduce baking soda/powder by 25%. Too much causes excessive rising and stress. Most recipes need 1 tsp baking soda per cup of flour.
Use an oven thermometer to verify temperature. If running hot, reduce by 25°F. Lower and slower = more even baking.
Lower oven temperature to 325°F for first 15 minutes, then raise to 350°F. Initial cooler baking forms a flexible surface that won't crack.
Don't open door before 2/3 of baking time. Sudden temperature drops cause cracking as the cake structure contracts.

next_time_tips: Make batter slightly thinner - cracks often indicate too-stiff batter
Use appropriate pan size - batter should fill pan 1/2 to 2/3 full
Add 1-2 tablespoons of sour cream to batter for flexibility
Cover top loosely with foil if browning too fast
Use a cake strip to prevent doming and uneven baking

possible_causes: Underbaked - interior still soft and collapses after removal
Oven too hot - outside sets while inside remains raw
Too much sugar - absorbs moisture and makes structure weak
Too much leavening - over-rises then can't sustain the structure
Opened door during baking - disrupts rising and cooling

fixes.details: Use a serrated knife or cake leveler to create a flat surface. The sunken part can be trimmed away and filled with frosting.
Next time, bake until a thermometer reads 200-210°F in the center. Don't rely on appearance alone - interior must be fully set.
Too much sugar weakens the gluten structure. Use exactly as much as the recipe specifies. Don't add extra for sweetness - adjust frosting instead.
Cut baking soda/powder by 10-25%. Too much causes the cake to rise beyond what the structure can support, leading to collapse.
Leave cake in pan for 10-15 minutes after removing from oven. The pan supports it while the structure sets. Let it cool completely before flipping.

next_time_tips: Don't open oven door until at least 3/4 of baking time has passed
Use the correct pan size - too large and cake doesn't have support
Add a stabilizer like cornstarch (1 tbsp per cup flour)
Make sure leavening agents aren't expired
Let cake cool completely in pan before turning out

possible_causes: Oven hot spots - temperature varies in different areas of oven
Oven racks not level - pan tilts and settles unevenly
Crowded oven - poor air circulation around pans
Thicker batter on one side - poured unevenly into pan
Uneven heat distribution - usually a thermostat issue

fixes.details: Place an oven thermometer in different spots. Note the variations. Front, back, top, bottom - where's hottest? Rotate pans accordingly next time.
Place a level on the oven rack. It should be perfectly horizontal. If not, shim with a metal spacer to level the rack.
Halfway through baking, rotate pan 180°. If using multiple pans, swap shelf positions (top to bottom). This compensates for hot spots.
Don't crowd the oven. Leave at least 2 inches between pan edges and oven walls. Air needs to circulate for even baking.
Tap pan gently on counter to settle batter evenly. Use a spatula to level surface. Even layer = even baking.

next_time_tips: Use an oven thermometer permanently to track temperature variations
Bake one pan at a time if uneven baking persists
Use baking strips to prevent excessive browning of edges
Keep oven door closed except during planned rotations
Arrange racks before preheating for optimal air circulation

possible_causes: Not cooled completely - stores moisture while still warm
Humid environment - absorbs moisture from the air
Stored without proper sealing - exposed to humidity
Underbaked - interior hasn't set properly
Wrong storage method - plastic wrap directly on warm cake creates steam

fixes.details: Leave cake on wire rack until completely cooled to room temperature (at least 1-2 hours). Never store warm cake.
Wrap cooled cake in plastic wrap or place in airtight container AFTER it's cooled. This prevents humidity absorption while retaining moisture.
Place a silica gel packet or uncooked rice in storage container to absorb excess moisture in humid climates.
If cake is underbaked, return to oven for 5-10 more minutes next time. Underbaked cakes stay gummy regardless of cooling/storage.
In humid climates, store in airtight container in coolest part of your home. Consider using a dehumidifier in your kitchen.

next_time_tips: Bake a bit longer than the recipe suggests if you live in a humid area
Store in refrigerator (extends life and reduces stickiness)
Place parchment paper between cake layers for easier separation
Use a cake box with ventilation holes if storing at room temperature
Dust lightly with powdered sugar before storing to prevent sticking

unclear_problem.message:
I'd love to help! Could you describe your baking problem more specifically? Here are some common issues I can help with:

• 🏜️ Dry cake
• 📉 Flat cake
• 🔥 Burnt cake
• 💧 Undercooked cake
• 🌙 Cracked cake
• ⬇️ Sinking cake
• ⚖️ Uneven baking
• 🍯 Sticky cake

Tell me what happened, and I'll provide detailed solutions!

not_cake_issue.message: It sounds like you might be describing a baking issue, but I'm not quite sure. I specialize in cake problems! If you have a question about cakes, baking techniques, or recommendations, I'm here to help. What would you like to know?
